section.title: Samson's Marriage
section.sectionRationale: The angel's promise in chapter 13 now meets the man himself, and he is not what we expected. Samson the Nazirite goes down to a Philistine town, demands a Philistine wife, kills a lion by the Spirit, hides a secret, sets a riddle, and loses both the wager and the wife. In one movement we see the deliverer's gifting and his appetites tangled together, and the startling note that the LORD is working an occasion against the Philistines through it all.
section.thesis: Samson pursues his own eyes and appetite into a forbidden marriage, yet the Spirit of the LORD rushes on him and God quietly turns even his self-willed folly into an occasion to strike the enemies of His people.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the woman at Timnah and the hidden purpose of God, verses 1 to 4; the lion torn and the honey taken, verses 5 to 9; the feast and the riddle, verses 10 to 14; the wife's tears and the secret betrayed, verses 15 to 18; and the Spirit, the slaughter, and the lost bride, verses 19 to 20.

verses.text: One day Samson went down to Timnah, where he saw a young Philistine woman.
So he returned and told his father and mother, “I have seen a daughter of the Philistines in Timnah. Now get her for me as a wife.”

But his father and mother replied, “Can’t you find a young woman among your relatives or among any of our people? Must you go to the uncircumcised Philistines to get a wife?” But Samson told his father, “Get her for me, for she is pleasing to my eyes.”
(Now his father and mother did not know this was from the LORD, who was seeking an {{toanah|occasion}} to move against the Philistines; for at that time the Philistines were ruling over Israel.)

units.label: The lion and the honey
units.note: out of the strong, something sweet

verses.text: Then Samson went down to Timnah with his father and mother and came to the vineyards of Timnah. Suddenly a young lion came roaring at him,

and the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD came powerfully upon him, and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands as one would tear a young goat. But he did not tell his father or mother what he had done.
Then Samson continued on his way down and spoke to the woman, because she was pleasing to his eyes.
When Samson returned later to take her, he left the road to see the lion’s carcass, and in it was a swarm of bees, along with their honey.

So he scooped some honey into his hands and ate it as he went along. And when he returned to his father and mother, he gave some to them and they ate it. But he did not tell them that he had taken the honey from the lion’s carcass.

units.label: The feast and the riddle
units.note: let me tell you a riddle

verses.text: Then his father went to visit the woman, and Samson prepared a feast there, as was customary for the bridegroom.
And when the Philistines saw him, they selected thirty men to accompany him.
“Let me tell you a {{chidah|riddle}},” Samson said to them. “If you can solve it for me within the seven days of the feast, I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty sets of clothes.
But if you cannot solve it, you must give me thirty linen garments and thirty sets of clothes.” “Tell us your riddle,” they replied. “Let us hear it.”
So he said to them: “Out of the eater came something to eat, and out of the strong came something sweet.” For three days they were unable to explain the riddle.

units.label: The secret betrayed
units.note: you would not have solved my riddle

verses.text: So on the fourth day they said to Samson’s wife, “Entice your husband to explain the riddle to us, or we will burn you and your father’s household to death. Did you invite us here to rob us?”

Then Samson’s wife came to him, weeping, and said, “You hate me! You do not really love me! You have posed to my people a riddle, but have not explained it to me.” “Look,” he said, “I have not even explained it to my father or mother, so why should I explain it to you?”
She wept the whole seven days of the feast, and finally on the seventh day, because she had pressed him so much, he told her the answer. And in turn she explained the riddle to her people.

Before sunset on the seventh day, the men of the city said to Samson: “What is sweeter than honey? And what is stronger than a lion?” So he said to them: “If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have solved my riddle!”

units.label: The Spirit and the lost bride

verses.text: Then the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon, killed thirty of their men, took their apparel, and gave their clothes to those who had solved the riddle. And burning with anger, Samson returned to his father’s house,
and his wife was given to one of the men who had accompanied him.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. Twice in this chapter the ruach of the LORD rushes powerfully on Samson, verses 6 and 19, so that he tears the lion and strikes down thirty men. The word underlines that Samson's strength is not his own muscle but the Spirit's gift, given even to a flawed and self-willed man for the deliverance of God's people.

toanah.gloss: An occasion, opportunity, or pretext. Verse 4 reveals that the marriage Samson demands is, unknown to his parents, from the LORD, who is seeking a toanah to move against the Philistines. The word shows God working through, not around, Samson's desires, bending even a sinful choice to His purpose to break Philistine oppression.

chidah.gloss: A riddle, a puzzling saying meant to test the hearer. Samson's chidah about the lion and the honey drives the drama of the feast; it is a private secret turned into a wager, and its betrayal exposes both his cleverness and the fragility of his self-reliance, setting the pattern of secrets and betrayal that will finally undo him with Delilah.

basic.context: Samson, set apart from birth as a Nazirite to begin delivering Israel from the Philistines, goes down to the Philistine town of Timnah and demands a Philistine woman as his wife. His parents object, but he insists, because she pleases his eyes. On the way the Spirit of the LORD rushes on him and he kills a lion barehanded; later he finds honey in its carcass and eats it. At the wedding feast he sets a riddle drawn from that secret. Under threat, his bride weeps it out of him and betrays the answer to her people, and Samson, cheated of his wager, kills thirty men of Ashkelon in his rage and storms home. His wife is then given to another man. Behind it all, verse 4 tells us, God is quietly working an occasion against the Philistines.
basic.hermeneutics: This is honest history, not a model to imitate. Samson's demand for a forbidden wife, his broken secrets, his temper, and his violence are described, not commended. Yet the narrator plants a crucial clue in verse 4: this was from the LORD. God's sovereign purpose to strike the Philistines does not excuse Samson's sin, but it is woven right through it. We must hold both together: a deeply flawed man, and a faithful God working even through his folly.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on three words. Twice the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, rushes on Samson so that his strength is plainly a gift, not his own. In verse 4 God is seeking a toanah, an occasion or pretext, against the Philistines, working through Samson's very desires. And the feast revolves around a chidah, a riddle, a secret made into a wager, whose betrayal foreshadows the greater secret Delilah will later drag out of him.
basic.typology: Samson is a deliverer raised up by God, empowered by the Spirit, who strikes the enemies of God's people, and in this he faintly points to Christ. But the likeness is by contrast as much as comparison: where Samson is ruled by his eyes and his appetite, Christ is the sinless Deliverer who conquers not by grasping but by giving Himself. The lion torn open yielding sweetness hints, however faintly, at strength and death made to yield life.
basic.christ: Samson shows us how badly Israel needs a Saviour who is more than a strong man. He is a Spirit-empowered deliverer, yet enslaved to his own desires; he begins to rescue Israel while wrecking his own life. Christ is the greater Deliverer of whom Samson is only a broken shadow: also anointed by the Spirit, also raised up to save His people, but holy where Samson is self-willed, and triumphing over the true enemy by His own perfect obedience rather than his impulses.
basic.god: The startling truth of the chapter is God's sovereignty. Verse 4 tells us the marriage Samson selfishly demanded was, unknown to everyone, from the LORD, who was seeking an occasion against the Philistines. God does not author Samson's sin, yet He is not thwarted by it; He bends even a self-willed choice to His saving purpose. And He pours out His Spirit on an unworthy man, showing that deliverance rests on grace, not on the deliverer's goodness.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 13 gives Samson's Nazirite calling and the promise that he would begin to save Israel. Genesis 50 shows God meaning for good what men mean otherwise. Romans 8 promises that God works all things together for good for His people. And Hebrews 11 lists Samson among those commended for faith, despite his failures.
basic.oneStory: In the dark days when everyone did what was right in his own eyes, God raised a flawed Nazirite to begin Israel's rescue. Samson's forbidden marriage, his riddle, and his rage all serve, in God's hidden hand, to strike the Philistines. It is one more step in the long story of a God who saves through weak and sinful instruments, pointing forward to the sinless Deliverer who alone can truly save.

context.literary: This begins the Samson cycle proper, the longest single account of any judge, and the last before the book collapses into chaos. The chapter is built on repeated motifs: going down, seeing, the pleasing of the eyes, the Spirit rushing, and hidden secrets. The riddle sits at its center, a puzzle the reader can solve because we alone were shown the lion and the honey, drawing us into Samson's private world and its dangers.
context.historical: Timnah was a Philistine border town in the Sorek valley, close to Samson's home region of Dan. Marriage to a Philistine violated Israel's calling to be separate, and the Philistines, a sea people with iron and five walled cities, dominated Israel at this time. A seven-day wedding feast with thirty companions and a wager of expensive linen garments reflects the customs of such celebrations.
context.canonical: Samson belongs to the downward spiral of Judges, where even the deliverers are increasingly compromised, exposing Israel's desperate need for a righteous king and a true Saviour. His Spirit-empowerment links him to the pattern of the judges, while his moral failure looks ahead to the anarchy of the book's final chapters and, beyond that, to the coming of Christ, the Deliverer who does not fail.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To open the Samson story by showing a Spirit-gifted but self-willed deliverer, and to teach, through verse 4, that God is sovereignly working even through Samson's compromised choices to begin Israel's rescue from the Philistines.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Samson's demands, deceptions, and violence; it does not prescribe them. His marriage to a pagan wife against his parents and his covenant, his rage, and his revenge are set before us as warnings, not examples. What endures is the truth of God's sovereign grace working through, and despite, a flawed man.

debates.question: Since verse 4 says the marriage was from the LORD, is Samson excused for pursuing a Philistine wife?

views.label: God's sovereignty over Samson's sin, without excusing it
views.case: Verse 4 speaks of God's hidden purpose, not His approval. Scripture regularly shows God ordaining occasions through sinful human choices, as with Joseph's brothers, Genesis 50:20, while still holding the sinners responsible. Samson's demand remains a violation of his calling; God overrules it without endorsing it.

views.label: A providential exception in Samson's unique calling
views.case: Some argue that because God specifically raised Samson to provoke the Philistines, this particular alliance served a divinely appointed role, so the usual prohibition is, in this case, suspended by God's direct purpose.

debates.bearing: The first reading is the safer and more consistent: God's sovereignty never launders human sin. Reading it so guards us from using God's providence to excuse our own disobedience, while still assuring us that He can redeem even our failures.

debates.question: Is Samson presented as a hero of faith or as a warning?

views.label: A genuine, if deeply flawed, man of faith
views.case: Hebrews 11:32 names Samson among those commended for faith. For all his failures, his strength came by the Spirit, he acted against God's enemies, and his final prayer was answered; God truly worked through him.

views.label: Chiefly a cautionary figure
views.case: The narrative dwells relentlessly on his appetites, his broken vows, and his ruin, holding him up as a picture of Israel itself: gifted, called, yet enslaved to its own desires and sliding toward disaster.

debates.bearing: Both are true and belong together. Samson is a real instrument of God's deliverance and a sobering warning; he magnifies grace precisely because he so little deserved it, and he drives us to long for a better Deliverer.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words carry its meaning. Twice the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, comes powerfully on Samson, verses 6 and 19, so that the tearing of the lion and the slaughter at Ashkelon are plainly the Spirit's doing, not merely a strong man's feats; his power is gift, not achievement. Verse 4 lets us in on the secret the parents never knew: the whole marriage was from the LORD, who was seeking a toanah, an occasion or pretext, to move against the Philistines who ruled Israel. God is not reacting to Samson's choices; He is working through them, without authoring the sin in them. And the feast turns on a chidah, a riddle, born of the private secret of the lion and the honey. That secret is pried loose by tears and threats and betrayed, a small rehearsal of the great secret Delilah will one day drag from his heart. Spirit, occasion, riddle: gift, providence, and the peril of secrets, the threads that will run through the whole tragic, grace-laced story.

typology.type: Samson, the Spirit-empowered deliverer raised up to save Israel
typology.fulfillment: Christ, anointed by the Spirit without measure to save His people, yet holy where Samson was self-willed, Luke 4:18 and Acts 10:38.

typology.type: The riddle, out of the strong came something sweet
typology.fulfillment: The gospel pattern of life brought out of death and sweetness out of strength conquered, supremely at the cross, faintly foreshadowed here.

typology.type: God seeking an occasion against the enemy through the deliverer
typology.fulfillment: God striking the true enemy through Christ, disarming the powers at the cross, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: Samson is a deliverer clothed with the Spirit, raised to rescue Israel, and in that role he points toward Christ. But he points as much by contrast as by likeness. Samson is mastered by what pleases his eyes; Christ came doing not His own will but the will of the Father, John 6:38. Samson begins Israel's rescue while ruining himself; Christ accomplishes our rescue by giving Himself. Both are anointed by the Spirit, Luke 4:18, both strike at the enemy of God's people, both bring, in a shadowy way, sweetness out of what was strong and deadly. Yet only Christ is the sinless Deliverer, and Samson's very failures make us ache for Him. Where Samson conquers in rage and appetite, Christ conquers in perfect love and obedience, and where Samson loses his bride, Christ wins His.
study.god: The controlling truth of the chapter is the sovereignty of God. Verse 4 pulls back the curtain: the marriage Samson selfishly demanded was, unknown to all, from the LORD, who was seeking an occasion against the Philistines. God does not author the sin in Samson's choice, yet He is in no way frustrated by it; He weaves even self-willed folly into His saving plan, exactly as He did with Joseph's brothers. And He pours His Spirit on an undeserving man, so that Samson's strength preaches grace: deliverance depends not on the goodness of the deliverer but on the good pleasure of God. He is sovereign, gracious, and patient, working steadily for the good of a people who scarcely deserve it.

crossReferences.note: Samson's Nazirite calling from the womb and the promise that he would begin to deliver Israel from the Philistines.
God meaning for good what men intend otherwise, the pattern behind verse 4's this was from the LORD.
God works all things together for the good of those who love Him, even through human failure.
Samson listed among those who by faith were made strong, despite his glaring failures.

study.oneStory: In the days when everyone did what was right in his own eyes, God raised a Spirit-gifted but self-willed Nazirite to begin Israel's deliverance. Samson's forbidden marriage, his riddle, and his rage all serve, under God's hidden hand, to strike the Philistines who oppressed His people. It is one more chapter in the long account of a God who saves through weak and sinful instruments, and it deepens the longing that runs through all of Scripture for the sinless Deliverer, Jesus, who alone can truly and finally save.

quiz.q: Why did Samson insist on marrying the woman of Timnah despite his parents' objection?

options.text: Because she was pleasing to his eyes, though unknown to all it was also serving the LORD's purpose against the Philistines.
options.note: Verse 3 gives his reason, she pleased his eyes, and verse 4 reveals God's hidden purpose behind it.

options.text: Because the LORD had openly commanded him to marry her.
options.note: God was sovereignly working an occasion, but Samson acted on his own desire, not an open command.

options.text: Because his parents finally chose her for him.
options.note: The opposite: his parents objected, and he overruled them.

options.text: Because she was a faithful Israelite woman.
options.note: She was a Philistine of Timnah, which is exactly why his parents objected.

quiz.q: What was the source of Samson's strength when he tore the lion apart?

options.text: The Spirit of the LORD, who came powerfully upon him.
options.note: Verse 6 credits the Spirit of the LORD, showing his strength was a gift, not merely his own.

options.text: His years of training and unusual muscle.
options.note: He was strong, but the text pointedly attributes the feat to the Spirit rushing on him.

options.text: A weapon he happened to be carrying.
options.note: He tore the lion with his bare hands; no weapon is mentioned.

options.text: The help of his companions at the feast.
options.note: He was alone at the vineyards, and the companions came only later at the feast.

quiz.q: How was Samson's riddle finally solved?

options.text: His wife, under threat, wept the answer out of him and betrayed it to her people.
options.note: Verses 15-17 show the threat, her tears, and the betrayal of the secret.

options.text: The thirty companions were clever enough to reason it out.
options.note: They could not solve it themselves; they had to extract the answer through his wife.

options.text: Samson accidentally revealed it while boasting.
options.note: He resisted telling it; it was pried out of him by pressure, not careless boasting.

options.text: His parents told the Philistines the answer.
options.note: His parents did not even know the secret of the lion and the honey.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 tell us about God's role in these events?

options.text: God was sovereignly seeking an occasion against the Philistines, working through Samson's choices without excusing his sin.
options.note: Verse 4 reveals God's hidden purpose while Samson remains responsible for his desires.

options.text: God was surprised by Samson's actions and had to adjust His plan.
options.note: The verse shows the opposite: God was already at work through the situation, not caught off guard.

options.text: God approved of every choice Samson made.
options.note: God's sovereign purpose does not mean approval; Samson's demand still violated his calling.

options.text: God had nothing to do with these events.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly says this was from the LORD.

quiz.q: How does Samson, the Spirit-empowered deliverer, point us to Christ?

options.text: As a flawed shadow: also anointed by the Spirit to save, but Christ is the holy Deliverer who conquers by obedience where Samson was ruled by his appetites.
options.note: Samson points to Christ by contrast as well as likeness; only Christ is the sinless Saviour.

options.text: As a perfect example whose life we should copy in every way.
options.note: Samson is Spirit-empowered, but his self-will and violence are warnings, not a pattern to copy.

options.text: As proof that strong human effort is enough to save.
options.note: His strength was the Spirit's gift, and his failures show human strength cannot save.

options.text: As a figure with no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: As a Spirit-anointed deliverer of God's people, he clearly foreshadows, however faintly, the true Deliverer.

questions: Samson was ruled by what pleased his eyes, overriding his parents and his calling. Where are your own desires quietly steering you away from what God has said?
Twice the Spirit rushed on Samson, yet his heart stayed self-willed. How do you guard against mistaking God's gifts in your life for God's approval of your choices?
Verse 4 shows God working His purpose even through Samson's compromised decision. How does it comfort you, without excusing sin, that God can weave even your failures into His good plan?
Samson kept dangerous secrets and set a riddle that would undo him. Is there a hidden area of your life that could become the crack through which you are betrayed or wrecked?
Samson's brokenness makes us long for a better Deliverer. How does seeing Christ as the sinless, self-giving Saviour, unlike Samson, move your worship this week?

translationNotes.note: This was from the LORD renders a phrase making God's hidden providence the cause behind the occasion against the Philistines; the verse does not sanction the marriage but reveals God's overruling purpose.
When the Philistines saw him carries a BSB footnote noting the sense may be when the bride's parents saw him, literally when they saw him; either way the thirty companions are provided for the feast.

On the fourth day follows the reading of the Septuagint and Syriac; the Hebrew reads seventh, as the BSB footnote records; the fourth-day reading fits the seven-day feast and the wife's week of weeping.
If you had not plowed with my heifer is a proverbial taunt: the men only solved the riddle by working through Samson's wife, exposing the betrayal.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 14:1-20. Poetic riddle lines joined inline with surrounding prose. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon set to none; no verified expository sermon on this passage confirmed this session.
